patterns.name: Agent-Build Course
patterns.summary: A structured course that walks builders through the four agentic design patterns: reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent collaboration. Each pattern is paired with code labs. Graduates leave able to build and ship a working agent.
patterns.intent: Graduate a builder who can identify, implement, and combine the four foundational agentic design patterns in a working, deployed agent.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a builder has basic Python literacy and wants a systematic introduction to agent design patterns, frameworks, and real deployments. It is the right choice over a single-framework or single-vendor tutorial.

inputs.name: Builder with basic Python literacy
inputs.description: A learner who can read and run Python scripts and understands API calls; no prior agent-framework experience required.

inputs.name: Agentic design pattern curriculum
inputs.description: Structured materials covering the four foundational patterns — reflection, tool use, planning, multi-agent — each paired with a runnable code example in a real framework such as LangGraph, CrewAI, or smolagents.

inputs.name: Framework sandbox environment
inputs.description: A pre-configured coding environment (Jupyter, Colab, or local venv) where each lab can be run without dependency friction.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can recognize, explain, and implement all four foundational agentic design patterns and compose them in a single agent system.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a deployed working agent
outputs.description: A shipped agent that uses tool-call and at least one other design pattern against a real task — the concrete proof the move was completed.

outputs.name: Completion credential
outputs.description: A certificate of completion from the course provider (Hugging Face, DeepLearning.AI, IBM Coursera, Microsoft Learn) that signals builder-level competence to employers.

steps.name: Learn each design pattern in isolation
steps.description: Work through reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent as separate modules. Each module pairs a conceptual explanation with a runnable code example so the builder can see the pattern in action before combining it with others.

steps.name: Build a pattern-composed agent
steps.description: Combine at least two patterns — typically tool use plus reflection or tool use plus planning — into a single working agent aimed at a real task. This step forces the builder to understand pattern interaction, not just individual mechanics.

steps.name: Complete capstone and earn credential
steps.description: Ship the finished agent through an assessment gate: a graded assignment, benchmark leaderboard (such as GAIA in the Hugging Face track), or peer review. Receiving the credential marks the threshold.

principles: Patterns before frameworks — understanding what reflection or planning does is more durable than memorizing a single SDK's API.
Runnable beats readable — every concept must be paired with code the learner can run, break, and modify to understand it.
Capstone under real conditions — the final agent must run against a real task, not a toy dataset.
patterns.threshold: The builder ships one deployed agent that uses tool-call and at least one other agentic design pattern, and can explain why each pattern choice fits the task.
patterns.masterpiece: A deployed, working agent that integrates at least two of the four foundational agentic design patterns against a real task — submitted for assessment and earning the course completion credential.

known_uses.quote: build agentic design patterns: reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent workflows
known_uses.note: Taught by Andrew Ng; 31 video lessons, 7 code examples, vendor-neutral Python; released 2025

known_uses.note: Taught by Harrison Chase (LangChain CEO) and Rotem Weiss (Tavily CEO); intermediate, 9 lessons

known_uses.name: Hugging Face AI Agents Course
known_uses.quote: Learn to use established AI Agent libraries such as smolagents, LlamaIndex, and LangGraph.
known_uses.note: Free; certificate of completion; final project benchmarked on GAIA; 3-4 hrs/week over 4 units

known_uses.name: Design, Develop, and Deploy Multi-Agent Systems with CrewAI
known_uses.quote: Bridge that gap by turning prototypes like an automated code reviewer, a meeting co-pilot, and a deep researcher into production-ready systems.
known_uses.note: Taught by João Moura (CrewAI CEO); 4 modules covering planning, memory, guardrails, MCP, CI/CD

known_uses.name: IBM RAG and Agentic AI Professional Certificate
known_uses.quote: equips software engineers with advanced generative AI skills, focusing on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), autonomous AI agents, LangChain framework implementations, and production-grade AI system deployment
known_uses.note: 9-course professional certificate; 3-month timeline; frameworks include LangGraph, CrewAI, AG2, BeeAI, MCP

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of teaching only one framework so deeply that builders cannot transfer skills when the framework changes. Courses that lead with SDK syntax rather than design patterns produce builders who are stuck when the API is updated.

The anti-pattern of completing all video lessons without shipping the capstone agent. Without the build step, the builder gains pattern vocabulary but not pattern skill — the credential means nothing and the move was not completed.

sources.quote: build agentic design patterns: reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent workflows
Study AI Agents in theory, design, and practice.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Builder with Python literacy] --> B[Learn each pattern in isolation
reflection / tool use / planning / multi-agent]
    B --> C[Build a pattern-composed agent
at least two patterns on a real task]
    C --> D{Capstone assessment
GAIA / graded assignment / peer review}
    D -- Pass --> E[Credential issued
DeepLearning.AI / HF / IBM / MSFT]
    D -- Fail --> C
    E --> F[More capable learner
+ deployed working agent]

patterns.example_scenario: A backend engineer at a mid-size logistics company has been writing REST APIs for five years but has never touched an agent framework. She enrolls in the DeepLearning.AI Agentic AI course to understand what her team is being asked to build. In the reflection module she sees how an LLM can critique its own output and loop until quality passes a threshold. In the tool-use module she wires a web-search tool to a simple agent and watches it answer questions it could not answer from memory alone. The planning module shows her how a task is decomposed into sub-steps before execution — the pattern her team actually needs for their route-optimization use case. For the capstone she builds an agent that accepts a shipment manifest, queries a freight-rate API, scores routes using a reflection loop, and outputs a ranked recommendation. The agent combines tool use and reflection — two patterns working together — in 140 lines of Python. She submits it, it passes the graded assessment, and she earns the completion certificate. Her manager sees the credential and assigns her as the agent-architecture lead on the team's next project. The masterpiece is not the certificate; it is the deployed agent that handles a real logistics task.

patterns.name: Agent-Builder Dojo
patterns.summary: A short, intensive, small-group practice den lasting days to weeks. Builders repeatedly build real agents against real business problems under close facilitation. Measurable output — typically time-to-deploy or hours-saved — is the exit condition.
patterns.intent: Ship at least one production-candidate agent per participant in a compressed, high-accountability build environment where the facilitator unblocks rather than lectures.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a team of builders already has foundational pattern knowledge and needs hands-on repetition against their own real problems — not when participants still need concept introduction. A dojo is the right follow-on to a build course; it is the wrong choice as a first step for complete beginners.

inputs.name: Cohort of 6–20 builders with shared technical level
inputs.description: Participants who have at least basic familiarity with agent concepts and Python; ideally they have completed a build course or equivalent self-study. The shared technical baseline prevents the facilitator from splitting attention across wildly different starting points.

inputs.name: Real business problems from participants' own organisations
inputs.description: Each participant brings a specific, scoped problem from their own work context — not a toy problem. Solving real problems is what produces measurable outcomes and prevents the dojo from becoming academic.

inputs.name: Facilitator with agent-architecture depth
inputs.description: A practitioner who can unblock on architecture decisions, prompt structure, tool plumbing, and framework selection. The facilitator's role is reactive unblocking, not proactive lecturing.

inputs.name: Build-deploy infrastructure
inputs.description: Cloud access, API keys, and deployment tooling so participants can push working agents by end of each cycle, not just run them locally in notebooks.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who has shipped an agent under real constraints and can describe the design decisions, failure modes encountered, and the next iteration needed.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a production-candidate deployed agent
outputs.description: A running agent — built by the participant against their own real problem — with a measured outcome such as tasks automated, hours freed, or error rate reduced.

outputs.name: Aggregate outcome report
outputs.description: A post-cohort summary tracking time savings, task-automation counts, or other business-impact metrics across all participants — the evidence base for continuing investment in the dojo programme.

steps.name: Problem scoping session
steps.description: Each participant presents their real business problem to the group. The facilitator and peers help scope it to the minimum agent design that can be shipped within the dojo's time box. Problems that are too broad get narrowed; problems that are trivially solvable without agents are redirected.

steps.name: Build-deploy-review cycles
steps.description: Daily or weekly cycles in which each participant builds, deploys something runnable, and gets a short review from the facilitator and peers. The cycle rhythm creates accountability and surfaces blockers early. No lecture-only sessions are permitted; every session produces a runnable artefact.

steps.name: Facilitated unblocking
steps.description: When a participant is stuck — on architecture, prompts, tool integration, or deployment — the facilitator unblocks in real time. Unblocking sessions are public when the problem is likely shared; private when it is specific to one participant's stack.

steps.name: Capstone demo and measurement
steps.description: Each participant presents their shipped agent with a measured outcome. The demo is live — the agent runs during the presentation. Outcomes are recorded in aggregate. Participants who have not shipped are not considered to have completed the dojo.

principles: Real problems only — a dojo that allows toy problems produces toy skills.
Facilitation is unblocking, not teaching — any session that turns into a lecture has lost the dojo format.
Ship something runnable every cycle — an agent that only runs in a notebook has not been built.
Measure what changed — time saved, tasks automated, error rate reduced: the dojo's proof is a number, not a feeling.
patterns.threshold: Each participant has shipped at least one production-candidate agent with a documented, measured outcome by the final session.
patterns.masterpiece: A running, deployed agent built against a real business problem from the participant's own organisation, with a measured outcome — tasks automated, hours freed, or quality metric improved — demonstrated live at the capstone session.

known_uses.note: Build and deploy a real multi-agent LLM application as final project; 277+ lessons across 8 courses

known_uses.name: Agilefever Agentic AI BootCamp

known_uses.note: Capstone required for certificate; 300+ enterprises served

known_uses.name: NEC BluStellar Academy for AI — 20-Day Bootcamp
known_uses.quote: bootcamp program to acquire essential AI skills in 20 days of intensive training
known_uses.note: In-house Japanese program; lang: ja; open course plus company-specific embedding since 2013

known_uses.name: AVILEN AIエージェント研修
known_uses.quote: AIエージェントの仕組みを理解し、基本的なレベルのAIエージェントアプリの構築が可能になること
known_uses.note: Japanese corporate training; two-part: lecture + no-code Dify build; lang: ja

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of participants scoping down to problems that are safe and easy rather than real and hard. When the dojo permits toy problems, participants ship agents that do not survive contact with production and the measured outcome is zero.

The anti-pattern of the facilitator filling dead time with concept explanations. Once a session turns into a lecture, the dojo has become a classroom and the hands-on accountability structure collapses.

The anti-pattern of calling a Jupyter notebook that runs locally a 'deployed agent'. If the agent cannot be invoked by someone other than the builder without help, it has not been shipped and the dojo threshold has not been met.

sources.quote: AIエージェントの仕組みを理解し、基本的なレベルのAIエージェントアプリの構築が可能になること

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Cohort of builders
with real business problems] --> B[Problem scoping session
narrow to shippable scope]
    B --> C[Build-deploy-review cycle
runnable artefact every cycle]
    C --> D[Facilitated unblocking
architecture / prompts / tools]
    D --> E{Agent deployed
outside notebook?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Capstone demo
live agent + measured outcome]
    F --> G[More capable builder
+ production-candidate agent]

patterns.example_scenario: A financial services firm runs a 4-week internal Agent-Builder Dojo with 12 engineers who completed the company's agent build course six weeks earlier. Each engineer brings a real problem from their team. One engineer is targeting the manual process of reconciling daily trade confirmations — currently two hours of analyst time per day. In the scoping session, the facilitator helps her narrow the problem to a single agent that reads incoming confirmation PDFs, extracts key fields, and flags discrepancies against the internal ledger. By end of week one she has a local prototype. The facilitator unblocks a tool-plumbing issue where the PDF parser was returning inconsistent JSON — a 20-minute fix that would have cost her two days alone. By week two the agent is deployed in a test environment reading real PDFs. By week three it is in production on a 10-trade daily sample. At the capstone demo in week four she shows the agent running live against that day's confirmations: 47 trades reconciled in 4 minutes, two discrepancies flagged, zero analyst time. Measured outcome: 1.8 analyst hours saved per day. The masterpiece is the running reconciliation agent with a documented time saving — not a notebook, not a demo script, but a deployed system processing real trade data.

patterns.name: Teach the Failure Modes
patterns.summary: A training session that drills builders on where production agents break: tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, non-idempotent replay, and metrics blindness. Builders learn to design for failure from the start, not after deployment.
patterns.intent: Give builders a working mental model of how production agents fail so they instrument guards before deployment rather than discovering failure modes in production.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move immediately after a team has built their first working agents and is preparing to move them toward production. It is poorly timed before the builder has any agent-building experience — the failure modes are abstract without a codebase to apply them to. It is dangerously late if applied only after a production incident.

inputs.name: Builders with at least one working agent
inputs.description: Participants who have built and deployed an agent at prototype level so they can map each failure mode to their own code rather than to an abstract example.

inputs.name: Catalogue of canonical failure modes
inputs.description: A curated list of real production agent failures: tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, over-eager execution, false success, state desync, flaky tools, non-idempotent replay, permission creep, silent degradation, and metrics blindness.

inputs.name: Real production traces or logs
inputs.description: Actual traces or sanitised logs of the failure modes occurring in real systems — not slides describing what could go wrong but evidence of what did go wrong.

inputs.name: Sandbox environment for failure reproduction
inputs.description: A safe environment where builders can deliberately trigger each failure mode in their own or a provided agent and then implement a guard.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can name at least five canonical agent failure modes, explain the mechanism behind each one, and describe the corresponding guard or mitigation.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a guarded agent
outputs.description: The participant's agent — from the preceding build sprint — updated to include at least two production guards: for example, an idempotency check and a loop counter, or an output schema validator and an error-budget circuit breaker.

outputs.name: Team failure mode register
outputs.description: A shared document in which the team records the failure modes they reproduced, the guards they implemented, and any failure modes specific to their domain or stack.

steps.name: Enumerate and explain the canonical failure modes
steps.description: Walk through the full catalogue — tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, over-eager execution, false success, state desync, flaky tools, non-idempotent replay, permission creep, silent degradation, metrics blindness. For each, show a real production trace or log of it happening, not a slide about what could happen. The goal is recognition, not theory.

steps.name: Reproduce a failure in the sandbox
steps.description: Builders choose one failure mode and deliberately trigger it in a sandbox agent. Triggering a loop counter bypass, a non-idempotent write, or a hallucinated tool call in controlled conditions is qualitatively different from reading about it. Builders document what they observe: what the agent said, what it did, and what a user would have seen.

steps.name: Implement and verify a guard
steps.description: For the failure mode they triggered, each builder implements a guard: a loop counter, an idempotency token, an output schema check, a circuit breaker. They verify that the guard catches the failure mode when triggered again. The guard must be in code, not a comment or a reminder.

steps.name: Red-team or debrief
steps.description: Optionally, split into two teams: one builds an agent and the other tries to trigger any failure mode in it within a time box. Alternatively, run a debrief focused on the question: 'which of these failure modes will show up in your production agent first?' Each team leaves with a prioritised guard list.

principles: Show real traces, not slides — a sanitised production log is worth more than any hypothetical example.
Fail loudly in staging, not silently in production — every guard exists to surface a failure before a user sees it.
Reproduce before you guard — a guard written against a failure mode the builder has never seen is likely incomplete.
patterns.threshold: The builder can name at least five canonical failure modes, explain the mechanism behind each, and has added at least two guards to their own production-candidate agent.
patterns.masterpiece: The participant's production-candidate agent updated with at least two code-level guards — each targeting a specific failure mode they reproduced in the sandbox — plus a written entry in the team failure mode register documenting what was triggered and how it was mitigated.

known_uses.name: Agent Reliability Gap — 12 Early Failure Modes
known_uses.quote: Agents aren't mystical. They're planners + tools + policies wrapped in a loop. Reliability lives in the edges between those boxes.
known_uses.note: Practitioner writeup cataloguing 12 failure modes; widely cited in 2025 enterprise agent deployments

known_uses.name: From Failure Modes to Reliability Awareness in Generative and Agentic AI Systems
known_uses.quote: Awareness is treated not only as a diagnostic score but also as a strategic input for AI governance
known_uses.note: 11-layer failure stack; introduces awareness mapping as a maturity tool; integrates DCAM framework

known_uses.name: Hugging Face AI Agents Course — Bonus Unit 2: Observability and Evaluation
known_uses.quote: You want to ensure that your agents are performing reliably in production.
known_uses.note: Covers tracing, token-cost monitoring, LLM-as-judge, offline benchmarking; part of free cert track

known_uses.name: AIエージェント開発者養成講座実践コース — Trainocate Japan
known_uses.quote: 評価・改善・運用まで含めた一連の進め方を把握し、開発・導入をリードできる
known_uses.note: 3-day Japanese practitioner course; includes tracing, evaluation, and operational failure considerations; lang: ja

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of running the workshop without real traces and without the sandbox reproduction step. Builders who only read about failure modes acquire vocabulary but not recognition skill — the next time the failure mode appears in their code, they will not identify it.

The anti-pattern of writing a guard and not verifying that it catches the failure mode when triggered. Guards written from memory rather than from a reproduced failure are often off-target and give a false sense of safety.

sources.quote: Most agent failures are boring, not mysterious. They're the same categories we've dealt with for decades — just wrapped in a more flexible planner.
failures rarely occur in isolation but propagate across layers, creating cascading effects with systemic consequences

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Builders with a working agent] --> B[Enumerate canonical failure modes
real traces, not slides]
    B --> C[Choose one failure mode
trigger it in sandbox]
    C --> D[Implement guard in code
loop counter / idempotency / schema check]
    D --> E[Verify guard catches
retriggered failure]
    E --> F{All priority modes
covered?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Red-team or debrief
prioritised guard list]
    G --> H[More capable builder
+ guarded production agent]

patterns.example_scenario: A team of four backend engineers at a B2B SaaS company has just shipped their first customer-facing agent. It is an AI assistant that schedules meetings by reading calendar APIs and sending invites. The product manager wants to expand its permissions to include deleting declined invites. The team joins a half-day Teach the Failure Modes workshop run by a senior engineer who has operated agents in production for two years. She opens with a real trace from a previous project. The agent entered a retry loop after a flaky calendar API returned a 429 and made 400 API calls in 90 seconds. The provider silently rate-limited it — meeting invites appeared to send but never arrived. The failure mode: flaky tools combined with no loop counter and no idempotency token on retries. Each engineer picks a failure mode from the catalogue and triggers it deliberately in the sandbox agent. One engineer triggers a non-idempotent replay by running the scheduler twice on the same input; it creates duplicate invites. She adds an idempotency token keyed on the meeting hash and confirms the duplicate is suppressed on re-run. Another engineer adds a loop counter that stops retries after five attempts and raises an exception that pages the team. By the end of the session, the team has added four guards to their agent and each guard has been verified against a deliberately triggered failure. The masterpiece is their updated scheduler agent. It now has idempotency, a loop counter, schema validation on the calendar API response, and an error-budget circuit breaker. They also add a two-page entry to the team's failure mode register.

patterns.name: Show the Working
patterns.summary: A training module that teaches builders to surface agent reasoning and action traces to end users — via logs, step-by-step summaries, or the explain-then-act pattern. Users can then verify agent behaviour and trust the output enough to adopt it.
patterns.intent: Teach builders to instrument their agents with human-readable reasoning traces so end users can verify agent behaviour without reading code or logs.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move whenever an agent's output will be acted upon by a human who did not write the agent — which is almost every production use case. Apply it before the agent reaches end users; retrofitting observability after a trust incident is harder and often too late to prevent adoption failure.

inputs.name: Builders with a working agent
inputs.description: Participants who have a prototype or production agent and are about to expose it to end users or stakeholders who need to trust its output.

inputs.name: Tracing tool access
inputs.description: An observability tool the builders can instrument during the workshop — OpenTelemetry, Langfuse, or LangSmith are common choices. Access needs to be configured before the session.

inputs.name: End-user acceptance test criteria
inputs.description: A clear definition of what a non-technical end user needs to be able to read in a trace to confirm the agent behaved correctly. This prevents the workshop from producing traces that only the builder can interpret.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can instrument any agent with structured reasoning traces, write human-readable step summaries, and design an explain-then-act interaction pattern.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: an agent with a readable trace
outputs.description: The participant's agent updated to expose a human-readable trace or step summary to the end user — not just the final answer — verified by a non-technical tester who can confirm the agent behaved correctly without reading code.

outputs.name: Instrumented agent codebase
outputs.description: The agent's source code with tracing calls added, covering the three log layers: context (what the agent knew), reasoning (why it chose), and actions (what it did).

steps.name: Explain the trust gap and the three log layers
steps.description: Open with the core problem: over 65% of organisations cite lack of explainability as the primary barrier to AI adoption. Introduce the three log layers — context (what the agent knew), reasoning (why it chose), and actions (what it did) — as the minimum readable trace structure. Show examples from real agents: one with no trace, one with a developer-only log, and one with a human-readable summary.

steps.name: Demonstrate explain-then-act pattern
steps.description: Show an agent built with the explain-then-act pattern: before taking any action, the agent outputs a brief reasoning statement that the user can read and optionally interrupt. Walk through the implementation. Show where in the agent loop the reasoning step is inserted and how it is formatted for non-technical readers. Show how to add a human-in-the-loop gate for high-stakes actions.

steps.name: Instrument an existing agent
steps.description: Builders add tracing calls to their own agent using the chosen tool. The goal is to cover all three log layers. The instrumentation must produce output that a non-technical person can read — not just raw JSON or framework debug logs. Builders test their traces by reading them aloud and checking whether a colleague who did not write the agent can follow what happened.

steps.name: Non-technical user acceptance test
steps.description: Each builder recruits a non-technical tester — a colleague, product manager, or workshop participant from another team — to read the trace and confirm whether the agent behaved correctly. The tester records any trace elements they could not understand. Builders revise until the tester can confirm correct behaviour without asking the builder to explain.

principles: A trace that only the builder can read has not been written for the user.
Trust is earned by showing reasoning before the user has to ask — explain-then-act, not act-then-defend.
Three log layers are the minimum: context, reasoning, actions — missing any one leaves the user unable to verify the full decision.
patterns.threshold: The builder's agent exposes a readable trace or step summary that a non-technical user can interpret without help, and that user can confirm whether the agent behaved correctly.
patterns.masterpiece: An instrumented agent that exposes a three-layer structured trace (context, reasoning, actions) to its end user — verified by a non-technical tester who can confirm correct behaviour without asking the builder to explain any part of it.

known_uses.name: AI Agent Observability — Building Trust Through AI Transparency
known_uses.quote: AI observability is the very foundation of AI trust. It's how you move from hoping your AI agent is behaving and performing correctly to knowing it is.
known_uses.note: Platform vendor; describes activity logs, audit logs, deployment logs as trust infrastructure

known_uses.name: Transparency and Explainability in Agentic AI Decision-Making
known_uses.quote: By exposing the internal logic of agentic decisions, various security teams can validate that agents are operating within their ethical and operational guardrails.
known_uses.note: Describes explain-then-act pattern and three-layer semantic audit trail (context / reasoning / actions)

known_uses.name: Hugging Face AI Agents Course — Bonus Unit 2
known_uses.quote: Monitor Metrics: Track performance indicators such as token usage (costs), latency, and error traces.
known_uses.note: Free curriculum unit; teaches OpenTelemetry instrumentation with smolagents; LLM-as-judge evaluation

known_uses.name: AgentTrace: A Structured Logging Framework for Agent System Observability
known_uses.quote: capturing a rich stream of structured logs across three surfaces: operational, cognitive, and contextual
known_uses.note: 2026 research framework; defines cognitive (reasoning) surface separately from operational (actions)

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of producing traces that are readable to the builder but not to the end user — raw JSON, framework debug output, or token-level logs. Traces written for developers do not close the trust gap for non-technical users and the adoption barrier remains.

The anti-pattern of adding observability instrumentation after a production incident rather than before first user exposure. Retrofitted traces are often incomplete because the original code structure did not preserve the reasoning state needed to populate the three log layers.

sources.quote: AI observability is the very foundation of AI trust.
By exposing the internal logic of agentic decisions, various security teams can validate that agents are operating within their ethical and operational guardrails.
instruments agents at runtime with minimal overhead, capturing a rich stream of structured logs across three surfaces: operational, cognitive, and contextual

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Builder with a working agent] --> B[Explain trust gap
+ three log layers]
    B --> C[Demonstrate explain-then-act
reasoning before action]
    C --> D[Instrument agent
OpenTelemetry / Langfuse / LangSmith]
    D --> E[Non-technical
user acceptance test]
    E --> F{User can confirm
correct behaviour?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[More capable builder
+ instrumented readable agent]

patterns.example_scenario: A product team at a legal-tech startup has built an AI contract review agent that flags missing clauses and drafts suggested additions. The agent works well in demos, but adoption by the firm's paralegal team has stalled. The paralegals do not know why the agent flags something as missing. They cannot defend the output to supervising attorneys. The team runs a Show the Working workshop. In the first step, the lead engineer shows the team three versions of a trace. The first is the raw LLM response chain — unreadable. The second is the framework debug log — developer-readable but cluttered with token counts and internal state. The third is a hand-crafted step summary she wrote for the demo — readable, but not produced systematically. She introduces the three-layer model: context (the contract clauses the agent read), reasoning (why it judged a clause to be missing), and actions (what it drafted). In the instrumentation step, two engineers add Langfuse tracing to the agent in 45 minutes. They structure the trace output as a human-readable checklist: 'Reviewed: NDA clause — present. Reviewed: limitation of liability — missing (standard threshold clause not found). Drafted: suggested limitation of liability text.' A paralegal from the firm who attended the workshop reads the trace cold and can identify which clause was missing and why. She tells the engineers: 'I can show this to the attorney and explain it. Before I couldn't.' The masterpiece is the instrumented agent with the three-layer trace. The real proof is the paralegal's ability to defend the output to a senior attorney without the engineers in the room.

patterns.name: Platform Agent Certification
patterns.summary: A vendor-issued credential (Salesforce, Microsoft, Google) that certifies a builder can design, configure, and deploy agents on that platform, providing a measurable threshold and a recognised signal for employers.
patterns.intent: Certify that a builder can design, configure, and deploy agents on a specific vendor platform, creating a credential that is meaningful to employers and clients who use that platform.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a builder works primarily within a single vendor ecosystem (Salesforce, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud) and needs a formal, employer-recognised signal of platform-level competence. It is the right choice when the organisation has standardised on a vendor platform and agent builders need a common, auditable skill baseline. It is the wrong choice as the sole training path — vendor certification certifies platform configuration, not agent design thinking.

inputs.name: Builder working in a vendor ecosystem
inputs.description: A practitioner who uses or is expected to use the vendor's agent tooling in their role — Salesforce Agentforce, Azure Foundry Agent Service, or Google ADK and Vertex AI Agent Builder.

inputs.name: Vendor learning path
inputs.description: The structured self-paced materials on the vendor's learning platform: Salesforce Trailhead Agentblazer path, Microsoft Learn AI-3026 modules, or Google Skills Agentic AI path.

inputs.name: Vendor agent-builder tooling access
inputs.description: A sandbox or developer account on the platform so the builder can complete hands-on labs rather than only watch demonstrations.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can configure, deploy, and monitor agents within the specific vendor platform — including the platform's tool integration model, agent orchestration primitives, and deployment pipeline.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a working vendor-platform agent
outputs.description: An agent deployed on the vendor platform that demonstrates the capabilities tested in the certification: tool calling, orchestration, multi-agent coordination (where applicable), and monitoring.

outputs.name: Vendor-issued digital credential
outputs.description: A named certification badge (Salesforce Certified Agentforce Specialist, Microsoft AI-3026 completion, Google ADK Technical Expert Badge) that can be shared on professional profiles and is recognised by employers using that platform.

steps.name: Complete the vendor learning path
steps.description: Work through the structured modules on the vendor learning platform. Each module covers a specific capability: agent configuration, tool definition, orchestration, memory, guardrails, and deployment. Complete all hands-on labs in the vendor sandbox — skip no labs, as the exam or capstone tests lab-level knowledge.

steps.name: Build a platform-native agent
steps.description: Design and deploy an agent using the vendor's native tooling — not a framework wrapper. For Salesforce this means Agentforce configuration in the Agent Builder UI and Flow. For Microsoft this means Foundry Agent Service with MCP tool definitions. For Google this means ADK agent code deployed on Vertex AI. The agent must use at least two platform-specific capabilities not available in framework-neutral code.

steps.name: Pass the assessment gate
steps.description: Complete the vendor's assessment: a proctored exam (Salesforce), a learning-path completion check (Microsoft), or a capstone project submission (Google Skills). Earn the digital badge. The badge is the threshold; the agent built in step two is the masterpiece.

principles: Platform depth complements pattern breadth — a vendor cert is most valuable when the builder already understands agent design patterns independent of the platform.
Labs before lectures — the exam tests what you can configure, not what you can describe; skip no hands-on exercises.
The credential is a signal to employers, not proof of agent design skill — treat it as a floor, not a ceiling.
patterns.threshold: The builder passes the vendor assessment gate and earns the named digital credential: Salesforce Certified Agentforce Specialist, Microsoft AI-3026 path completion, or Google ADK Technical Expert Badge.
patterns.masterpiece: A working agent deployed on the vendor platform that uses at least two platform-specific capabilities — demonstrated during the capstone or exam lab — earning the vendor-issued digital credential.

known_uses.name: Salesforce Certified Agentforce Specialist
known_uses.quote: Certified Agentforce Specialists are responsible for managing and optimizing Agentforce and have deep understanding of both Salesforce platform configuration and Agentforce capabilities.
known_uses.note: Formerly AI Specialist; free first attempt until Dec 2025; requires Agentblazer Trailhead path

known_uses.name: Develop AI Agents on Azure (AI-3026)
known_uses.quote: By the end of this learning path, you will have the skills needed to develop AI agents on Azure.
known_uses.note: 9-module learning path; covers Foundry Agent Service, custom tools, MCP, multi-agent orchestration, A2A protocol; free on Microsoft Learn

known_uses.name: Agentic AI on Google Cloud
known_uses.quote: accelerate knowledge exchange, integrate with enterprise databases, and deploy secure, advanced AI agent solutions on Google Cloud
known_uses.note: 9 activities; covers Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform (ex-Vertex AI), ADK, multi-agent security; technical expert badge

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of completing a platform certification without understanding the underlying agent design patterns. A builder who can configure Agentforce but cannot explain why a reflection loop or a planning pattern would or would not suit their use case cannot adapt when the platform changes its UI or when the requirement does not fit the vendor's agent model.

The anti-pattern of making the platform certification the organisation's only agent training, producing builders who cannot work outside the vendor's tooling. When the vendor changes its architecture, the team's skills are stranded.

sources.quote: Certified Agentforce Specialists are responsible for managing and optimizing Agentforce
you will have the skills needed to develop AI agents on Azure
accelerate knowledge exchange, integrate with enterprise databases, and deploy secure, advanced AI agent solutions on Google Cloud

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Builder in a vendor ecosystem
Salesforce / Azure / Google] --> B[Complete vendor learning path
Trailhead / Microsoft Learn / Google Skills]
    B --> C[Build a platform-native agent
using vendor-specific capabilities]
    C --> D[Pass assessment gate
exam / capstone / path completion]
    D --> E{Credential earned?}
    E -- No --> B
    E -- Yes --> F[Digital badge issued
Agentforce Specialist / AI-3026 / ADK Expert]
    F --> G[More capable builder
+ vendor-platform agent deployed]

patterns.example_scenario: A Salesforce administrator at a mid-market insurance company has been configuring Flows and custom objects for three years. Her manager asks her to lead the firm's Agentforce rollout. The target agent handles tier-one policy enquiries: it looks up policy data, answers coverage questions, and routes complex claims to a human agent. She has never built an AI agent. She starts the Agentblazer Trailhead path, working through the Agent Builder configuration modules over two weeks in her spare time. She completes all lab exercises in her developer sandbox. These cover creating an agent topic, defining actions backed by Flows and Apex, setting agent instructions, and configuring guardrails that block any action writing to policy records. In the third week she builds the policy enquiry agent in the full Trailhead environment. It retrieves policy data via a custom Apex action and answers coverage questions from a curated knowledge base. When the caller mentions a claim, a Flow routes the interaction to a human agent. She runs it against 20 test cases from real tier-one queries her team has logged. 17 pass without escalation. She registers for and passes the Certified Agentforce Specialist exam. Her digital badge is issued and she adds it to her LinkedIn profile. Within a month, three colleagues from other Salesforce shops reach out asking for advice on their own Agentforce rollouts. The masterpiece is the production-deployed policy enquiry agent — not the badge, which is the threshold signal that she is ready to be trusted with it.

patterns.name: Framework Deep-Dive
patterns.summary: A focused course on a single agent-orchestration framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, smolagents, AutoGen/AG2) that takes a builder beyond hello-world into state management, memory, human-in-the-loop, streaming, and production deployment.
patterns.intent: Take a builder from hello-world familiarity with a framework to production-level competence — including state management, memory, human-in-the-loop patterns, streaming, and deployment.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move after a builder understands the foundational agentic design patterns and has chosen — or been assigned — a specific framework as their team's standard. It is the wrong starting point for beginners. The abstractions make most sense once a builder already knows what a planning loop or a tool call does and wants to see how this specific framework implements them.

inputs.name: Builder with foundational agent design pattern knowledge
inputs.description: A practitioner who has completed a build course or equivalent and understands what reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent mean — independent of any one framework's syntax.

inputs.name: Chosen framework
inputs.description: The specific orchestration framework the course targets: LangGraph (state graphs, nodes, edges), CrewAI (crews, agents, tasks, processes), smolagents (CodeAgent, ToolCallingAgent, multi-agent hierarchies), or AutoGen/AG2 (conversational agents, group chats).

inputs.name: Framework documentation and course materials
inputs.description: The official framework documentation plus a structured course — such as LangChain Academy's Introduction to LangGraph or Deep Agents with LangGraph — that progresses from core model to production deployment.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can design and implement stateful, multi-turn agents with memory, streaming, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and multi-agent orchestration within the chosen framework — and who knows the framework's production deployment options.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a stateful production agent
outputs.description: A deployed, stateful, multi-turn agent with at least one tool integration and one human-in-the-loop checkpoint — demonstrating that the builder has moved past hello-world into production-ready use of the framework.

outputs.name: Reusable project code
outputs.description: A set of runnable projects — one per module — that the builder can extend and adapt for their own production use cases, serving as a personal reference implementation library.

steps.name: Module 1: Core graph or agent model
steps.description: Understand the framework's fundamental abstraction: LangGraph's state graph with nodes and edges; CrewAI's crew, agent, and task hierarchy; smolagents' CodeAgent and tool registry; AG2's conversational agent and group chat. Build a minimal runnable agent that exercises the core model without any advanced features.

steps.name: Module 2: Memory and persistence
steps.description: Add short-term memory (conversation history, scratchpad) and long-term memory (vector store or database-backed) to the agent. Understand the framework's checkpointing model — how agent state is saved and restored across sessions. Build a multi-turn agent that remembers context between invocations.

steps.name: Module 3: Human-in-the-loop and streaming
steps.description: Add human-in-the-loop hooks: approval gates before high-stakes actions, interrupt-and-revise patterns, and feedback injection. Add streaming so the agent's intermediate steps are visible to the user in real time. Build an agent that streams its reasoning and pauses for human approval before any write operation.

steps.name: Module 4: Multi-agent orchestration
steps.description: Add a second agent — a subagent, a specialist, or a critic — and wire the two together using the framework's orchestration primitives. Understand routing, handoff, and shared state between agents. Build a two-agent system where the orchestrator delegates to the specialist and synthesises the result.

steps.name: Module 5: Deployment and monitoring
steps.description: Deploy the agent to a production-ready environment using the framework's deployment tooling: LangGraph Platform, CrewAI Enterprise, or a cloud container. Add observability: traces, error logs, and a basic dashboard. The module ends with a live endpoint that can be called from outside the developer's machine.

principles: Understand the framework's core abstraction before touching advanced features — a builder who skips the state model will misuse memory and streaming.
Every module ends with a runnable project — reading documentation without building is preparation, not learning.
Deploy before the course ends — a framework skill that has never produced a live endpoint has not been validated.
patterns.threshold: The builder deploys a stateful, multi-turn agent with at least one tool integration and one human-in-the-loop checkpoint on the chosen framework's production deployment tooling.
patterns.masterpiece: A live, externally callable stateful agent — built in the chosen framework, deployed to a production environment, with memory across turns, at least one human-in-the-loop checkpoint, and at least one real tool integration — that any team member can invoke without the builder's help.

known_uses.name: Foundation: Introduction to LangGraph — LangChain Academy
known_uses.quote: LangGraph helps developers add better precision and control into agentic workflows.
known_uses.note: Free; 55 lessons, 6 hours; builds agents with memory, streaming, human-in-the-loop, long-term memory, deployment

known_uses.name: Project: Deep Agents with LangGraph — LangChain Academy
known_uses.quote: shipping reliable agents faster
known_uses.note: Free; 30 lessons, 2 hours; builds subagents, file-handling agents, production-ready compositions

known_uses.name: AIエージェント開発者養成講座実践コース — Trainocate Japan
known_uses.quote: AIエージェントの基本概念、活用パターン、開発時の検討事項がわかる
known_uses.note: 3-day Japanese practitioner course; LangChain, LangGraph, Deep Agents; includes tracing and RAG; lang: ja

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of completing module one and moving on to a new framework rather than going deep. Builders who collect hello-world familiarity across four frameworks without mastering any one of them cannot deploy production agents in any of them.

The anti-pattern of completing modules one through four and never doing module five because 'deployment is a DevOps problem'. A framework skill that has never produced a live endpoint has not been validated under production conditions; the masterpiece has not been built.

sources.quote: Learn the basics of LangGraph - our framework for building agentic and multi-agent applications
the fundamental characteristics of Deep Agents and how to implement your own Deep Agent for complex, long-running tasks
AIエージェントの基本概念、活用パターン、開発時の検討事項がわかる

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Builder with pattern literacy
and a chosen framework] --> B[Module 1: Core graph or agent model
minimal runnable agent]
    B --> C[Module 2: Memory and persistence
multi-turn stateful agent]
    C --> D[Module 3: Human-in-the-loop + streaming
approval gate + real-time trace]
    D --> E[Module 4: Multi-agent orchestration
two-agent delegation system]
    E --> F[Module 5: Deployment and monitoring
live endpoint + observability]
    F --> G{Externally callable
agent deployed?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[More capable builder
+ stateful production agent]

patterns.example_scenario: A machine learning engineer at a media company has read about LangGraph, run a few notebook examples, and understands in abstract what a state graph is. Her team has been asked to build a research assistant agent that gathers sources, summarises them, and lets an editor review and approve each summary before publication. She decides to do the LangChain Academy Introduction to LangGraph course to get past hello-world. Module one gives her the state graph mental model: nodes as processing steps, edges as routing logic, state as the typed dict that flows through the graph. She builds a minimal three-node graph (search, summarise, output) that works end-to-end. Module two adds memory: the agent now stores previous search sessions and does not re-fetch sources it has already processed. Module three adds the human-in-the-loop checkpoint her editor workflow requires. After each summary the graph pauses; the editor reads it and approves or requests a revision. The graph then continues, or re-runs the summarise node with the revision note injected. She adds streaming so the editor sees the summary appearing word by word rather than waiting for the whole output. Module four introduces a critique subagent that scores each summary for factual accuracy before it reaches the editor — a quality gate that reduces editor revision requests. Module five deploys the graph to LangGraph Platform with a REST endpoint, a LangSmith tracing integration, and an error alert on graph exceptions. The editor can now invoke the agent from a simple web form without the engineer's help. The masterpiece is the live research-assistant agent: stateful, human-in-the-loop approved, streaming, two-agent, production-deployed — and callable by the editorial team without a Python environment.

patterns.name: Center-of-Excellence AI-Native Engineering
patterns.summary: An enterprise Center of Excellence dedicated to training internal builders to design, build, and run agents on the company's own stack. Vendor technical-enablement partners support the CoE. The goal is a standing agent-building capacity across the organisation, not one-off experiments.
patterns.intent: Build a standing internal capacity to design, deploy, and operate agents at enterprise scale by training cohorts of engineers inside a dedicated CoE with vendor or specialist technical-enablement support.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when an organisation has passed the proof-of-concept stage and needs repeatable agent-building capability across multiple teams — not when the first prototype is still being built. It requires an executive mandate, a dedicated team of 5–20 engineers, and at least one vendor or specialist partner willing to provide deep technical enablement, not just account management.

inputs.name: Executive mandate and CoE team
inputs.description: Leadership commitment to fund a dedicated team of 5–20 engineers for a sustained period, with a mandate to build production agents — not to evaluate them.

inputs.name: Vendor or specialist technical-enablement partner
inputs.description: An AI provider (such as Anthropic, OpenAI, Microsoft, or Google) or specialist consultancy that provides architecture guidance, code review, and failure-mode reviews to the CoE team — not just sales support or generic documentation.

inputs.name: Real production problems from internal stakeholders
inputs.description: A backlog of real automation opportunities from business units across the organisation — the raw material that each CoE cohort builds against.

inputs.name: Internal platform: reference architectures, prompt libraries, evaluation harnesses
inputs.description: A growing internal knowledge base that the CoE builds and maintains — reusable components that successive cohorts extend rather than rebuild from scratch.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: Each CoE cohort graduate who has built and shipped a production agent — and who returns to their home team as a local agent-building expert able to mentor colleagues without CoE support.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a portfolio of production agents with measurable outcomes
outputs.description: A growing portfolio of agents built by CoE graduates, each with a documented business outcome — tasks automated, hours freed, cost reduced — that constitutes the organisation's evidence base for continued investment.

outputs.name: Internal reference architecture
outputs.description: A documented, tested, organisation-specific agent architecture standard — covering stack choices, security model, data access patterns, and observability — adopted by teams outside the CoE.

outputs.name: Distributed agent-building experts
outputs.description: CoE-trained engineers embedded in business units across the organisation who can scope, build, and operate agents without returning to the CoE for every new project.

steps.name: Stand up the CoE with a mandate and partner
steps.description: Secure executive sponsorship with a clear charter: the CoE produces production agents, not evaluations or strategy documents. Engage the technical-enablement partner and agree on what support they will provide — architecture reviews, office hours, failure-mode audits. Staff the CoE with engineers who have or can rapidly acquire foundational agent-building skills.

steps.name: Run rolling cohort embeds
steps.description: New builders from across the organisation embed with the CoE for 4–12 weeks. Each cohort member builds a real agent against a problem from their home team under CoE mentorship. The CoE lead reviews architecture decisions; the technical-enablement partner reviews for security, scalability, and failure modes. Cohort members ship before they exit.

steps.name: Build and publish internal reference assets
steps.description: As each cohort runs, the CoE documents what it learns. This produces a reference architecture for the company's stack, a prompt library with versioned templates, and an evaluation harness that tests agents against a common quality standard. These assets accumulate over time and reduce the ramp-up cost for each successive cohort.

steps.name: Measure and report business outcomes
steps.description: Track the agents shipped by CoE graduates: what they automate, what they save, what quality improvements they produce. Report this to the executive sponsor. The CoE's continued mandate depends on this evidence; without it, the programme is indistinguishable from an expensive experiment.

principles: The CoE builds, not advises — a CoE that only produces strategy documents and internal presentations is not a builder programme.
Cohort graduates return as experts — the exit condition is not a certification but the ability to mentor a colleague in the home team without CoE support.
The vendor partner enables, not replaces — technical enablement from a vendor supplements CoE capability; the CoE retains architectural ownership.
Outcomes trump activity — the programme's evidence is production agents with measured business impact, not hours of training delivered or courses completed.
patterns.threshold: CoE graduates have each shipped a monitored production agent with a documented business outcome, and the internal reference architecture has been adopted by at least one team outside the CoE.
patterns.masterpiece: A portfolio of production agents built by CoE cohort graduates — each with a documented business outcome and monitored in production — plus an internal reference architecture standard adopted by teams outside the CoE, demonstrating that the organisation now has standing agent-building capacity rather than isolated expertise.

known_uses.name: NEC / Anthropic AI-Native Engineering CoE
known_uses.quote: NEC will establish an internal Center of Excellence (CoE) with the aim of developing highly skilled AI professionals, utilizing technical support and training provided by Anthropic.
known_uses.note: 30,000 NEC employees on Claude globally; CoE targets one of Japan's largest AI-native engineering teams; announced April 2026

known_uses.name: NTT DATA OpenAI Center of Excellence
known_uses.quote: NTT DATA Launches Smart AI Agent™ to Accelerate Generative AI Adoption
known_uses.note: CoE serves as knowledge hub for OpenAI-stack agent implementation; targets $2B agent revenue by 2027; tens of thousands of GenAI practitioners

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of a CoE that produces guidelines, evaluation reports, and governance frameworks but does not ship production agents. An advisory CoE builds institutional authority but not institutional capability; the builder programme threshold is not met.

The anti-pattern of a CoE whose entire architecture expertise resides in the vendor technical-enablement team rather than in the CoE engineers. When the vendor relationship changes, the CoE's capability evaporates. Technical enablement must transfer knowledge, not just solve problems.

The anti-pattern of cohort members who embed with the CoE but do not ship an agent before returning to their home team. Without a shipped agent as the exit condition, the embed produces exposure but not capability, and the distributed-expert goal is not reached.

sources.quote: NEC will establish an internal Center of Excellence (CoE) with the aim of developing highly skilled AI professionals, utilizing technical support and training provided by Anthropic.
NTT DATA Launches Smart AI Agent™ to Accelerate Generative AI Adoption and Drive $2 Billion in Revenue by 2027

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Executive mandate
+ vendor enablement partner] --> B[Stand up CoE team
5-20 engineers with charter]
    B --> C[Rolling cohort embeds
4-12 weeks per cohort]
    C --> D[Build real agent against
home-team problem]
    D --> E[CoE + vendor architecture review
+ failure-mode audit]
    E --> F{Agent shipped
before cohort exit?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Graduate returns as
home-team expert]
    G --> H[CoE publishes reference
architecture + prompt library]
    H --> I[Measure business outcomes
report to executive sponsor]
    I --> C

patterns.example_scenario: A Japanese technology integrator with 40,000 engineers is running scattered AI experiments across 30 business units but cannot get any agent past the proof-of-concept stage. Each team that builds something hits the same architecture problems, solves them independently, and cannot share the solution because there is no common platform. The CTO secures a board mandate: stand up a CoE with a two-year runway and a goal of 50 production agents shipped by end of year one. The company engages Anthropic as the technical-enablement partner. Anthropic provides three things. First, architecture office hours with CoE engineers every two weeks. Second, a code review of every agent before it moves to staging. Third, a failure-mode review checklist tailored to the company's security and compliance constraints. The first 12-engineer CoE cohort runs for eight weeks. Each engineer brings a real problem from their home team. By week six, all 12 have agents in staging. By week eight, nine are in production — the other three are blocked by data-access approvals outside the CoE's control, not by engineering failure. The nine who ship document their architectures in a shared internal wiki. By month three, the wiki holds enough patterns for the CoE to publish a reference architecture. It covers stack choices: Claude API, LangGraph, internal auth, and Datadog for observability. The CoE also publishes a prompt template library for the most common internal tasks and an evaluation harness that runs 50 standard test cases against any new agent before deployment. Cohort two's ramp time is 40% shorter because they start from the reference architecture rather than blank files. By the end of year one, 47 agents are in production across 18 business units. The masterpiece is not any individual agent but the portfolio. It contains 47 agents, each with a documented outcome, owned by engineers in business units who built them without CoE dependency. The reference architecture is what made cohort two's ramp 40% shorter than cohort one's.
